answer:
In Allama Iqbal's *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, the poem "Sitara" (chapter 22)—translated as "The Morning's Star" and referring to Venus—personifies the star as weeping in dread over its transient existence.[^1] The speaker first quotes the star's lament:

> The Morning's Star was weeping, filled with dread,  
> "I gained my sight, but not to see," it said.  
>   
> The sun's own breath gives life to all I see,  
> But dawn's hem grants no refuge unto me.[^2]

The star describes its own station as fleeting: "What is the station of a star so bright? / A bubble's breath, a spark's brief flash of light."[^3]

The speaker then addresses Venus compassionately as "O jewel on the brow of day," inviting it to descend from the heavens into the eternal garden of poetry: 

> If ruin grieves you, come from heav'n away.  
> Descend from heaven's height with morning dew,  
> My garden of verse has life to offer you.  
>   
> I am its gardener, Love its season's prime,  
> Its form is built to be as old as time.[^4]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 22 ("Sitara"), [translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/22-sitara/translation/).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 22 ("Sitara"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/22-sitara/translation/#p-eb2ea6).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 22 ("Sitara"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/22-sitara/translation/#p-9ed297).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 22 ("Sitara"), [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/22-sitara/translation/#p-b6423f).
